era_context: The early modern period: the Protestant Reformation is consolidating in Northern Europe; the Ottoman Empire under Süleyman the Magnificent is expanding; the Spanish Empire is consolidating control in Europe and expanding in the Americas; the Ming Dynasty rules China under the Jiajing Emperor; the Mughal Empire under Humayun is facing challenges.

events.title: Pilgrimage of Grace (English rebellion against Henry VIII)
events.description: The Pilgrimage of Grace, a major rebellion against Henry VIII's religious reforms and dissolution of the monasteries, began in October 1536 in northern England. The rebellion involved thousands of participants, including nobles, clergy, and commoners, and represented significant opposition to Henry's break with Rome and his religious policies. The rebellion was eventually suppressed by 1537, with numerous rebels executed. The Pilgrimage of Grace was one of the most significant challenges to Henry VIII's authority and had lasting implications for English religious and political development.
events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in English royal archives and contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Dissolution of the Monasteries (England)
events.description: Henry VIII's dissolution of the monasteries accelerated in 1536, with the Crown seizing monastic lands and properties. The dissolution, which had begun in 1536 and continued through the 1540s, was a major religious and economic transformation in England. The dissolution resulted in the destruction of monastic institutions, the redistribution of lands to the Crown and nobility, and significant social disruption. The dissolution was a key component of Henry's break with Rome and his establishment of the Church of England.

sources.name: English royal records and administrative documents

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in English royal archives and contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Ottoman-Venetian conflict in the Mediterranean
events.description: Ottoman forces under Süleyman the Magnificent continued military operations against Venetian possessions in the Mediterranean in 1536. Ottoman naval forces attacked Venetian territories in the Aegean and Adriatic, demonstrating Ottoman naval superiority. These conflicts were part of a broader Ottoman expansion in the Mediterranean and would eventually lead to the Treaty of Constantinople in 1540, in which Venice ceded significant territory to the Ottomans.
events.certainty_note: Well-documented in Venetian and Ottoman sources.

events.title: Spanish conquest of Cartagena (Colombia)
events.description: Spanish conquistador Pedro de Heredia founded the city of Cartagena in present-day Colombia in 1536. Cartagena became an important Spanish colonial port and administrative center in South America. The founding of Cartagena was part of broader Spanish expansion in South America and the consolidation of Spanish colonial control over the Caribbean coast.

events.title: Sher Shah Suri's rise to power in India
events.description: Sher Shah Suri, an Afghan ruler, began his rise to power in 1536, challenging Mughal Emperor Humayun's authority in northern India. Sher Shah's rise represented a significant challenge to Mughal dominance and would culminate in Humayun's defeat and temporary exile in 1540. Sher Shah's rule (1540–1545) would be marked by administrative reforms and the construction of the Grand Trunk Road.
events.certainty_note: General timeline and participants confirmed, but specific events in 1536 are less distinctly documented.

events.title: Ming Dynasty military operations against wokou
events.description: The Ming Dynasty continued military operations against wokou (Japanese pirates) along the Chinese coast in 1536. These raids, which had intensified in the 1530s, prompted the Ming government to strengthen coastal defenses and reorganize naval forces. The wokou raids were a persistent problem throughout the 16th century and prompted significant military and administrative reforms in coastal provinces.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: General context of wokou raids confirmed; specific operations in 1536 are less distinctly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The Pilgrimage of Grace (1536-001) is sometimes portrayed as a purely religious rebellion; modern scholarship emphasizes that it was also motivated by economic grievances, social disruption, and opposition to centralized royal authority. The dissolution of the monasteries (1536-002) is sometimes dated to 1536 as the beginning; modern scholarship emphasizes that the process was gradual and that significant dissolutions occurred both before and after 1536. The founding of Cartagena (1536-004) is sometimes attributed to 1533 or other dates in different sources; the exact date of founding versus formal establishment is debated.
historiographic_note: The year 1536 is well-documented in European sources, particularly for English, Ottoman, and Spanish events. English royal archives provide extensive documentation of the Pilgrimage of Grace and the dissolution of the monasteries. Ottoman and Venetian sources provide detailed accounts of their military conflicts. Spanish colonial records document the founding of Cartagena. Chinese sources are abundant but focus primarily on court politics and military operations. Indian sources are less abundant and often rely on later compilations. The historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented, particularly in non-European regions.

graph_edges.note: Pilgrimage of Grace was suppressed in 1537 with numerous executions
